brief_document.title: read_history, list_active, and query over libSQL
brief_document.purpose: Implement the read side of the contract: reconstruct a workflow's history from stored blobs, list non-terminal workflows, and translate a WorkflowFilter into SQL returning summaries. Status and summaries must be derived from event rows via aion-core's projection — never a mutable stored status column — so the durable store cannot drift from the event log.
brief_document.task: Implement read_history, list_active, and query in read.rs. read_history range-scans the events table for a workflow and deserialises each blob in seq order. list_active and query derive WorkflowStatus and WorkflowSummary from the lifecycle event rows using aion-core's projection. query translates WorkflowFilter (type, status, time range, parent) into a WHERE clause. No mutable status table.

requirements.title: read_history
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL return a workflow's complete event history in ascending seq order, deserialising each stored blob into an Event.
acceptance: read.rs implements read_history(&self, &WorkflowId) -> Result<Vec<Event>, StoreError> as an indexed range scan ordered by seq ascending.
Each row's event blob is deserialised via serde; deserialisation failure maps to StoreError::Serialization.
An unknown workflow returns the documented result from AC-005/AC-006 (empty history vs NotFound) — match the trait docs exactly and assert that behaviour in a test.
A test appends events then asserts read_history returns them in the order they were appended, byte-for-byte equal after round-trip.

requirements.title: Status projection from event rows
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL derive WorkflowStatus for a workflow by projecting over its lifecycle event rows using aion-core's projection. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT store or read a mutable status column.
acceptance: read.rs derives status by reading the workflow's lifecycle events and applying aion-core's status projection (the last lifecycle event determines status), identical to how InMemoryStore derives it.
No schema column or in-memory field holds a mutable status that is written independently of the events (design constraint CO7).
A test appends a WorkflowStarted then a WorkflowCompleted and asserts the derived status is Completed; a test with only WorkflowStarted asserts Running.
The projection logic is shared between list_active and query (single derivation path, not duplicated).

requirements.title: list_active
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL return the WorkflowIds whose projected status is non-terminal (Running).
acceptance: read.rs implements list_active(&self) -> Result<Vec<WorkflowId>, StoreError> returning workflows whose projected status is Running.
Workflows with a terminal lifecycle event (Completed/Failed/Cancelled/TimedOut) are excluded.
A test creates running and terminal workflows and asserts list_active returns exactly the running ones.
The implementation reads only what it needs to project status (does not deserialise full histories for every workflow when a targeted lifecycle-row query suffices).

requirements.title: query — WorkflowFilter to SQL and summaries
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL translate a WorkflowFilter (workflow type, status, time range, parent) into a SQL WHERE clause and return matching WorkflowSummary values, each derived from lifecycle event rows.
acceptance: read.rs implements query(&self, &WorkflowFilter) -> Result<Vec<WorkflowSummary>, StoreError> building the WHERE clause from the filter's set fields; an empty/default filter imposes no constraints and returns all workflows.

Filtering by workflow type matches WorkflowStarted's recorded type; by status matches the projected status; by time range matches against recorded_at / start time; by parent matches the parent workflow id recorded for child workflows.
Each returned WorkflowSummary carries workflow id, type, status, start time, and end time, derived from the WorkflowStarted row and the terminal event row (status via the projection from R2).
Query parameters are bound (parameterised SQL), not string-interpolated, so values cannot break the query; a test asserts a filter value containing SQL metacharacters is handled safely.

The secondary index defined in AS-002 R3 is now validated against the concrete query/list_active plan: if the finalised WHERE/ORDER columns differ from what AS-002 indexed, this brief amends schema.rs to match (schema.rs is in this requirement's modify set for exactly that reason — do not leave a wrong or unused index).
Tests cover each filter dimension individually and a combined filter, matching the equivalent assertions the AC-007 conformance suite makes.

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement append (AS-004) or timer methods (AS-006).
SHALL NOT introduce a mutable status column or workflow record — status and summaries are projections over event rows.
SHALL NOT interpret payloads — only the columns the store filters/orders on are first-class; payloads stay opaque (D2).
SHALL NOT string-interpolate filter values into SQL — parameters must be bound.
verification: A reviewer confirms no mutable status column exists and that status derivation matches aion-core's projection used by InMemoryStore.
A reviewer confirms query uses bound parameters, not string interpolation.
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: read_history returns a workflow's complete event history in ascending seq order, deserialised from the stored blobs.
list_active returns the WorkflowIds whose projected status (via aion-core's projection) is non-terminal (Running).
query translates a WorkflowFilter (workflow type, status, time range, parent) into a SQL WHERE clause and returns matching WorkflowSummary values.
WorkflowStatus and WorkflowSummary fields are derived from lifecycle event rows via aion-core's projection; there is no mutable stored status column.

stories.text: As the engine, I want a workflow's complete history to survive process restarts so that I can replay it from durable storage after a crash or reboot.
As the engine, I want to list active workflows and durable timers from storage on startup so that I know what to replay and which timers are due after downtime.
As an operator, I want to query durable workflows by type, status, and time range so that I can see what is running and what has failed without loading every full history.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny. No unsafe in the crate.
No #[allow]/#[expect]/#[ignore] bypasses per CLAUDE.md. Tests needing a remote primary use a runtime env-gate, not #[ignore].
lib.rs is declarations and re-exports only; logic lives in named modules.
500-line file limit (excluding tests/comments/whitespace). The EventStore impl is split across append, read, and timer modules.
Depends only on aion-store (and transitively aion-core) among Aion crates — no dependency on the engine, beamr, or any other backend. Structural; must hold.
Raw SQL is permitted here because this crate IS a storage backend (the storage layer). No other crate issues SQL against this store; they go through the EventStore trait.
WorkflowStatus is derived from event history via aion-core's projection. No mutable stored status field; a cached projection must never diverge from the events.
LibSqlStore must pass the shared AC-007 conformance suite unmodified — the same behavioural standard as the oracle.
append's sequence guard and event inserts execute in a single libSQL transaction; a mismatch writes nothing and returns SequenceConflict — no partial batch is ever persisted.
LibSqlConfig is Deserialize-only and carries no hardcoded durability/WAL defaults; tunable values are surfaced for the operator to set, per CLAUDE.md no-assumed-defaults.
All libsql and serde errors are mapped into the StoreError taxonomy at the crate boundary; no foreign error type leaks across the EventStore surface.

resolved_context.intention: When an engine operator embeds Aion and writes no storage configuration, this is the store they get: a single file on disk, zero external infrastructure, full durability. The same crate serves the distributed path — the local file becomes an embedded replica syncing to a remote primary — without the operator learning a second backend. The in-memory reference store (cluster AC) proves the contract but forgets on restart; LibSqlStore makes durability the easy default, held to the identical behavioural standard as the oracle. A workflow that sleeps for months and resumes needs its history to survive every crash and reboot in between. When this cluster is done, an operator writes LibSqlStore::open("app.db") and has a production-grade durable event store the engine cannot tell apart from the oracle except that it remembers; the shared AC-007 conformance suite passes against it unchanged, plus a durability-specific suite proving state survives close-and-reopen.
